data.title: Why Are Adobe Shares Sliding On Friday?
data.description:
Adobe Inc (NASDAQ:ADBE) shares fell on Friday. The Nasdaq fell 1.27% while the S&P 500 shed 0.97%.

Analyst Downgrade Hits Sentiment

On Thursday, William Blair analyst Arjun Bhatia downgraded Adobe. The rating moved from Outperform to Market Perform, according to Benzinga Pro.

This shift follows growing unease regarding the software sector.

Competition and ‘SaaSpocalypse’ Risks

Concerns are emerging in the SaaS sector as investors assess the potential impact of AI-driven automation on traditional subscription-based business models.

The confluence of AI-driven disruption fears, deteriorating technical setups, and a shift in fundamental valuation metrics has created a challenging environment for the stock. 

Google’s Stitch Poses Threat

If non‑designers can produce professional‑grade work through conversational prompts, demand for Adobe’s traditional design suite could weaken over time.

Technical Analysis

Adobe is trading 9.8% below its 20-day simple moving average (SMA)  and 22.8% below its 100-day SMA, which keeps both the short-term and intermediate trend pointed down. Shares are down 40.86% over the past 12 months and are sitting much closer to their 52-week low than their high.

The RSI is at 34.34, which sits in neutral territory but is leaning toward the oversold side after the late-February washout (RSI moved into oversold on 2026-02-19). MACD is at -9.3486 and remains below its signal line at -7.7782, a bearish configuration that suggests downside pressure is still dominating.

The combination of RSI in the 30–50 range and bearish MACD suggests mixed momentum.

  • Key Resistance: $255.00
  • Key Support: $233.50

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions

The stock carries a Buy Rating with an average price target of $344.90. Recent analyst moves include:

  • William Blair: Downgraded to Market Perform (Mar. 26)
  • Citigroup: Neutral (Lowers Target to $278.00) (Mar. 16)
  • Goldman Sachs: Sell (Lowers Target to $220.00) (Mar. 16)

ADBE Stock Price Activity: Adobe shares were down 2.60% at $234.62 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo by Mats-Wiklund via Shutterstock

data.title: A Mom Gave Up On Her Daughters After Spending Over $500K On Their College Because 'They've Turned Into Socialists'
data.description:
After spending more than $500,000 on her daughters' education, one mother says she no longer wants to leave them any inheritance.

Speaking on “The Ramsey Show,” Monique from Bangor, Maine said she's reached a breaking point after years of feeling uneasy about how to divide her assets between her two children.

A Growing Divide Over Values

Monique explained that her daughters ended up in very different financial situations. One is in the top 1% with her husband, while the other earns about $50,000 a year. But money isn't what's driving her decision.

Don't Miss:

“For several years I felt uncomfortable about how to divide up the assets,” she said. “But more recently I've gotten to the point where I don't want to give either of them any money.”

She pointed to the sacrifices she made over the years, especially paying for their college.

“I feel like I've worked so hard my whole life. I paid for college for them, I've paid over half a million dollars for college for them, and they've turned into socialists,” she said.

When personal finance expert Dave Ramsey burst out laughing, she said, “I know we’re all laughing, but frankly, I'm depressed. I'm very depressed about where I see my children heading.” 

“You shouldn’t [feel terrible],” Ramsey said. “They're not entitled. They didn't hit the DNA lottery.”

Ramsey said that parents are under no obligation to leave money to their children.

“If my children are going to be parasites, I'm not going to finance their lives,” he said.

Trending: Skip the Regrets: The Essential Retirement Tips Experts Wish Everyone Knew Earlier.

You Can Set Conditions  Or Walk Away

Monique asked whether she could structure a trust so her daughters wouldn't access the money until later in life, hoping they might change.

Ramsey said that's possible, but it doesn't address the bigger issue.

“Then they'd just be a 70-year-old socialist,” he said.

Instead, Ramsey explained that inheritance can be tied to values or conditions. He shared that his own estate plan is built around a trust with specific expectations.

“If they're not walking with God, they don't get to manage God's money,” he said.

While his approach is faith-based, he suggested Monique could adapt the same idea to reflect her own beliefs.

Co-host John Delony encouraged her to slow down and get more precise about what's bothering her.

“I would take some time to distill down beneath the word ‘socialist,'” he said. “I want you to have a direct conversation about what these things are.”

Delony warned against relying on labels and pushed for clarity instead. 

“You are not required morally, ethically, legally, spiritually to leave your money to people that you do not agree with how they live their lives,” Ramsey added.

From there, he suggested having a direct but respectful conversation.

“I don't agree with the way you're living your life, and I'm not willing to finance that going forward,” Ramsey said, offering an example of what she could say.

In the end, the message from both hosts was simple: inheritance isn't automatic. It's a personal decision, and parents can choose to tie it to their values or not leave it at all.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Semiconductor Chip Stocks Slump As Middle East War Hits Day 28: Trump Sets New Deadline
data.description:
Geopolitical tensions in the Middle East are weighing on tech stocks and disrupting semiconductor supply chains.

• NVIDIA stock is under selling pressure. What’s driving NVDA stock lower?

Chip Stocks Slide As Conflict Escalates

Tech and semiconductor stocks declined Friday as the U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict entered its 28th day, amid rising energy prices that are fueling inflation concerns.

Helium Shortage Disrupts Chip Supply Chain

The conflict has tightened global helium supply, a key input in chipmaking, pushing prices higher and disrupting production.

Companies are seeking alternative sources amid supply constraints and transport delays, Reuters reported on Thursday. Executives warned that prolonged shortages could lead to production cuts, affecting industries ranging from electronics to automobiles.

Cramer Flags Strong Nvidia Fundamentals

CNBC’s Jim Cramer said investors should focus on Nvidia’s fundamentals rather than geopolitical noise.

He noted the stock’s decline is not directly tied to the conflict and reflects its high trading liquidity. While higher rates and memory costs could pressure spending, he said strong demand for AI infrastructure continues to support Nvidia, adding that demand remains “incredibly strong.”

Price Actions: Nvidia shares were down 1.50% at $168.67, Broadcom shares were down 2.58% at $301.37, and Advanced Micro Devices shares were down 0.42% at $202.91 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: Activist Investor Urges Teleflex To Engage Potential Buyers
data.description:
Irenic Capital, which holds a 2% stake in Teleflex, has sent a letter to the board demanding a more open approach to evaluating strategic alternatives to maximize shareholder value.

Irenic Flags Value Erosion

The letter highlights a staggering 73% total shareholder return decline over the past five years, emphasizing the need for meaningful changes in leadership and board composition.

The letter also points out that the current board has failed to make open market purchases of stock, raising concerns about their commitment to shareholder interests.

Irenic argues that the board’s long tenure has led to a disconnect with shareholders, urging them to consider engaging with interested parties to explore potential acquisition offers.

Technical Analysis

Teleflex, a maker of surgical devices, is currently trading 3.7% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 6.7% above its 50-day SMA, indicating short-term strength.

However, it is trading 1.5% below its 200-day SMA, suggesting some long-term weakness, particularly as shares have decreased 16.62% over the past 12 months and are positioned closer to their 52-week lows than highs.

The RSI is at 51.75, which is considered neutral territory, indicating that the stock is neither overbought nor oversold at this time. Meanwhile, the MACD is at -1.1866, below its signal line of -0.8255, indicating bearish pressure on the stock.

The combination of neutral RSI and bearish MACD suggests mixed momentum for Teleflex, indicating that while the stock is not in an overbought or oversold condition, there are underlying bearish signals that traders should monitor closely.

  • Key Resistance: $124.00
  • Key Support: $102.50

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Hold Rating with an average price target of $167.00. Recent analyst moves include:

  • RBC Capital: Sector Perform (Raises Target to $125.00) (Feb. 27)
  • Wells Fargo: Equal-Weight (Raises Target to $130.00) (Feb. 27)
  • Needham: Buy (Raises Target to $147.00) (Feb. 26)

Teleflex Benzinga Edge Rankings

Below is the Benzinga Edge scorecard for Teleflex, highlighting its strengths and weaknesses compared to the broader market:

  • Value Rank: 17.69 — Trading at a steep premium relative to peers.
  • Growth Rank: 5.1 — Indicates weak growth prospects.
  • Quality Rank: 2.14 — Suggests poor quality metrics.
  • Momentum Rank: 14.25 — Indicates weak momentum.

The Verdict: Teleflex’s Benzinga Edge signal reveals a weak profile across key pillars, indicating challenges in value, growth, and momentum, which may concern investors looking for stability.

TFX Stock Price Activity: Teleflex shares were up 5.37% at $116.42 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image via Shutterstock

data.title: MARA Sells $1B In Bitcoin At A Loss To Pay Down Debt As Hedge Fund CIO Warns: 'Just The Beginning'
data.description:
The kicker: MARA disclosed separately that it bought 4,267 BTC in 2025 at an average price of around $111,000: roughly $45,000 above where the token trades today.

Lekker Capital CIO Calls It A ‘Major Unwind’

Quinn Thompson, CIO of hedge fund Lekker Capital, wrote on X that MARA is now a “distressed seller” and called this “just the beginning of a major unwind across the sector.”

That hashrate decline is healthy for the network long-term, but the near-term problem is that public miners hold close to 80,000 BTC on their balance sheets. As they become AI companies, they both need capital to fund the buildout and have no reason to hold Bitcoin anymore.

On-chain data from CryptoQuant backs him up. The Miners’ Position Index has climbed to a seven-day high, signaling a broader uptick in miner outflows beyond just MARA.

BTC Dropped Below $70K On The News

BTC closed below $70,000 on Thursday and extended losses today, falling roughly 4% to $66,200.

The average all-in cost of mining a single BTC currently sits around $78,600. CoinShares’ latest report called Q4 2025 the most challenging quarter since the April 2024 halving and estimated publicly traded miners may derive 70% of revenues from AI by year-end.

The Selling Is Sector-Wide

TeraWulf ended 2025 with 3 BTC on its balance sheet. IREN liquidates all mined Bitcoin daily. Cipher sold $214.7 million in BTC last year. MARA still holds 38,689 BTC after the sale, making it the largest remaining miner treasury and a potential source of further supply pressure.

On Polymarket, traders price a 51% chance BTC dips to $45,000, a 27% chance it hits $35,000 and only an 18% it regains $120,000 this year.

MARA stock is down almost 8% today.

Mike Novogratz recently argued that Bitcoin has been trading well through the war in Iran. Bitcoin miners may be about to change that.

Image: Shutterstock
